phrasal_verbs.meaning: stop people from entering an area by putting something such as a rope around it
move towards someone quietly and slowly, especially because you want to surprise them
move more slowly than other people so that you are behind them; make less progress or be less successful than other people who are doing a similar job or activity
become lost or go to the wrong place
prevent someone from going somewhere by getting in front of them; prevent something from taking place
stop someone or something from moving forwards
start living in a different house or flat (with)
leave one place and travel to another; stop discussing or doing something and begin discussing or doing something different
permanently leave the house or flat where you live or the place where you have your business
change your position in order to make space for someone or something
stop by the side of the road in a car or other vehicle
move so that sb can pass you; leave a job so that sb else can take over
visit somewhere before continuing to another place
(of an object) have one end move upwards, especially because something heavy has been put at the other end; turn a container upside down so that the things inside it come out
leave a meeting, performance, etc before the end, usually because you are angry or bored; suddenly leave a relationship, your family, your job, etc

head: head towards/for (a place)
head a ball
head a committee/etc
head a list
point: make a point of doing sth
way: get sth out of the way

idioms.meaning: in a straight line (used for measuring distance)
go straight forward, without turning; do what you feel is right in a particular situation, although you are not certain
a long way from any town or city
become confused about where you are and where other things are
go towards someone or something in the quickest and most direct way
away from populated areas or areas popular with tourists
suddenly stop, for example because you are surprised
follow a path that is quicker and shorter than the usual way
follow a longer path than the usual one because it is more attractive

A.title: Original course practice
A.instructions: Complete each original item using the target language from this lesson.

questions.sentence: He ...... his coat from the hook and rushed out into the freezing morning air.
She ...... in her bag for her keys, unable to find them as the rain began to fall.
The old man came through the hospital doors ...... a crumpled piece of paper with his appointment details on it.
The officer ...... to a seat at the back of the room and told the witness to wait.
The train manager ...... to signal that the doors were about to close.
In his frustration, he ...... the wall and then immediately regretted it.
Without looking up from her notepad, the doctor ...... towards the examination table.
She ...... her teeth as the dentist began to work, determined not to show any sign of discomfort.
He ...... the side of the boat firmly as the wake from a passing vessel rocked it violently.
The fox ...... along the garden wall and disappeared under the gate without making a sound.
After twisting her ankle on the steps, she had no choice but to ...... on one foot to the nearest chair.
He prefers to ...... for half an hour before breakfast rather than going to the gym.
If you'd like to ...... this way, the doctor will be with you shortly.
We ...... across the road just before the lights changed and barely made it to the other side.
The tunnel was so narrow that the rescue workers had to ...... on their stomachs to get through.
The veterans ...... in silence through the town centre as a mark of respect for those who had fallen.
The barrister ...... into the courtroom and placed her files firmly on the table without a word.
The goalkeeper ...... to his left and managed to push the ball around the post.
The children ...... along the seafront ahead of their parents, pointing excitedly at the boats.
The life raft had been ...... on the open water for two days before the coastguard helicopter finally spotted it.
In this wildlife reserve, herds of elephants are free to ...... across hundreds of square kilometres without fences.
After the seminar ended early, I ...... through the old town market for an hour before heading back to my hotel.
He ...... on the wet pavement outside the café and spilled his coffee all down his jacket.
The children discovered that the frozen hillside was the perfect surface to ...... down on their coats.
She ...... over the corner of the rug and dropped all the files she had been carrying.
When a dog ran out into the road, the cyclist braked sharply and ...... across the tarmac.

B.title: Original course practice
B.instructions: Complete each original item using the target language from this lesson.

questions.sentence: We ...... over the low wall at the edge of the car park and onto the hillside path beyond. By noon, we had ...... to a ridge with panoramic views across the valley. Clouds were building to the south, and our guide recommended we ...... by the shorter track before conditions deteriorated.

The harbour master's voice was calm and measured. The sailing vessel had lost all engine power and was ...... silently towards the rocks. Without sufficient ...... , there was no chance of steering clear. The only option was to ...... by releasing the spinnaker. She needed the wind to continue to ...... across the sail, or the bow would begin ...... dangerously low. As she ...... the harbour wall, she braced herself and prepared to abandon ship.
questions.answer: gliding, velocity, accelerate, flow, sinking, approached

questions.sentence: The Arctic tern ...... over seventy thousand kilometres each year in the longest migration of any living creature. Unlike seeds that simply ...... on ocean currents, this bird navigates by the stars and the angle of the sun. Scientists have yet to fully explain how each individual recalls its precise ...... across two polar regions year after year.

In tennis, the characteristics of a well-served ball depend heavily on the court surface. On grass, the ball tends to ...... low and fast, making it hard for the receiver to react in time. A skilled player with topspin can make the ball ...... as it travels through the air, dipping sharply before impact. As the ball ...... on its axis at pace, it grips the surface differently from a flat strike and can be virtually unreadable.

In the aftermath of the Second World War, thousands of families ...... from war-devastated parts of Europe to countries such as Canada, the United States and Australia. Life was often difficult for these ...... , who had to rebuild from scratch in unfamiliar cultures and languages. Many nations also opened their doors to ...... who had been forced from their homes by conflict or political persecution.
